title: SOC 2 CC6 — Encryption in Transit Policy Enforcement for Admin and API Paths

compliance.assurance_rationale: Auto-migrated from cat-22 markdown on Phase 1.3 run. Requires SME review to confirm clause accuracy, mode (satisfies vs detects-violation-of), and assurance level (contributing/partial/full).

dataSources: `index=network` `sourcetype IN ("zeek:ssl","nginx:access")` (server_name, ssl_version, cipher, cert_expires); tag admin paths via `uri_path`
app: Splunk Add-on for Zeek (Splunkbase 1617), Splunk Add-on for Nginx or F5 LTM (as applicable)
spl:
index=network sourcetype="zeek:ssl" earliest=-24h
| where ssl_version IN ("TLSv1","TLSv1.0","SSLv3") OR like(cipher,"%NULL%") OR like(cipher,"%EXPORT%")
| stats count by server_name, ssl_version, cipher
| sort - count
value: CC6 expects encryption of data in transmission. Detecting TLS policy regressions—weak cipher suites, certificate expirations, or plaintext admin sessions—provides continuous evidence that cryptographic controls remain effective.
implementation: (1) Expand to internal services behind load balancers using `server_name`; (2) join cert inventory for `cert_expires` within 30 days; (3) separate customer-facing vs admin VIPs; (4) integrate with vulnerability SLA for weak TLS; (5) document compensating controls for legacy apps with approved exceptions lookup.
visualization: Table (weak sessions), Line chart (trend of weak cipher hits), Single value (distinct servers).
cimSpl: | tstats summariesonly=t count from datamodel=Network_Traffic.All_Traffic by All_Traffic.action, All_Traffic.src, All_Traffic.dest, All_Traffic.dest_port | sort - count
knownFalsePositives: Administrative tasks, scheduled jobs or platform updates can match this pattern — correlate with change management, maintenance windows and user role before raising severity.
